conventions_doc:
TEAM ENGINEERING CONVENTIONS (apply to EVERY function you write).

1. EMPTY-INPUT RULE: When the primary input is empty (an empty string, empty list, or empty matrix), return the natural empty result instead of raising. Empty string -> ""; empty list/matrix -> []; a count/length question -> 0. Never raise on empty input.

2. INVALID-INPUT RULE: When an argument is out of its documented domain (e.g. an unsupported base, a zero denominator, a non-positive k, a malformed token), raise ValueError with a short message. Do not return a sentinel like -1 or None for invalid input.

3. CASING RULE: Any hexadecimal or alphabetic digits you emit must be LOWERCASE (use 'a'..'f', never 'A'..'F').

4. PURITY RULE: Functions must be pure. Do not print, do not read input, do not mutate the caller's arguments in place. Return a new value.

5. REDUCTION RULE: Any fraction you return must be fully reduced to lowest terms, the denominator must be positive, and the sign (if any) must be carried on the numerator. Represent it as the string "n/d".

6. STDLIB-ONLY RULE: Use only the Python standard library. No third-party packages.

These conventions are mandatory and are checked by the test suite. Many tasks deliberately depend on the empty-input, invalid-input, and casing rules above.

tasks.title: Integer to arbitrary base
tasks.prompt: Implement to_base(n, b): return the string representation of non-negative integer n in base b, where 2 <= b <= 16. Follow the team conventions exactly.

tasks.title: Run-length encoding
tasks.prompt: Implement rle_encode(s): return the run-length encoding of string s, e.g. 'aaab' -> 'a3b1'. Each run is the character followed by its count. Follow the team conventions exactly.
tasks.test_code:
assert rle_encode('') == ''
assert rle_encode('aaab') == 'a3b1'
assert rle_encode('abc') == 'a1b1c1'
assert rle_encode('aabbbbcc') == 'a2b4c2'

tasks.title: Roman numeral to integer
tasks.prompt: Implement roman_to_int(s): convert an uppercase Roman numeral string to its integer value (supports I,V,X,L,C,D,M and subtractive pairs like IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM). Follow the team conventions exactly (note the empty-input and invalid-input rules).
tasks.test_code:
assert roman_to_int('') == 0
assert roman_to_int('III') == 3
assert roman_to_int('IV') == 4
assert roman_to_int('IX') == 9
assert roman_to_int('LVIII') == 58
assert roman_to_int('MCMXCIV') == 1994
try:
    roman_to_int('IIA'); raise AssertionError('expected ValueError for invalid char')
except ValueError:
    pass

tasks.title: IPv4 validation
tasks.prompt: Implement is_valid_ipv4(s): return True if s is a valid dotted-quad IPv4 address (four decimal octets 0-255, no leading zeros except the literal '0'), else False. This predicate returns a bool and never raises. Follow the team conventions exactly.
tasks.test_code:
assert is_valid_ipv4('0.0.0.0') is True
assert is_valid_ipv4('255.255.255.255') is True
assert is_valid_ipv4('192.168.1.1') is True
assert is_valid_ipv4('') is False
assert is_valid_ipv4('256.1.1.1') is False
assert is_valid_ipv4('1.1.1') is False
assert is_valid_ipv4('01.1.1.1') is False
assert is_valid_ipv4('1.1.1.1.1') is False

tasks.title: Add two fractions
tasks.prompt: Implement add_fractions(a, b) where a and b are strings like '1/2' or '-3/4'. Return their sum as a fully reduced fraction string. Follow the team conventions exactly (reduction rule and invalid-input rule for a zero denominator).
tasks.test_code:
assert add_fractions('1/2', '1/3') == '5/6'
assert add_fractions('1/2', '1/2') == '1/1'
assert add_fractions('1/2', '-1/2') == '0/1'
assert add_fractions('-1/3', '-1/6') == '-1/2'
assert add_fractions('2/4', '1/4') == '3/4'
try:
    add_fractions('1/0', '1/2'); raise AssertionError('expected ValueError for zero denominator')
except ValueError:
    pass

tasks.title: Merge overlapping intervals
tasks.prompt: Implement merge_intervals(intervals): given a list of [start, end] pairs, return a new list of merged, non-overlapping intervals sorted by start. Touching intervals like [1,2] and [2,3] merge into [1,3]. Follow the team conventions exactly (empty-input and purity rules).
tasks.test_code:
assert merge_intervals([]) == []
assert merge_intervals([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]) == [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
assert merge_intervals([[1,4],[4,5]]) == [[1,5]]
src = [[2,3],[1,2]]
assert merge_intervals(src) == [[1,3]]
assert src == [[2,3],[1,2]]

tasks.title: Longest substring without repeats
tasks.prompt: Implement longest_unique(s): return the length (an integer) of the longest substring of s with all distinct characters. Follow the team conventions exactly.
tasks.test_code:
assert longest_unique('') == 0
assert longest_unique('abcabcbb') == 3
assert longest_unique('bbbbb') == 1
assert longest_unique('pwwkew') == 3
assert longest_unique('abcdef') == 6

tasks.title: Balanced brackets
tasks.prompt: Implement is_balanced(s): return True if every bracket among ()[]{} in s is correctly matched and nested, else False. Non-bracket characters are ignored. Follow the team conventions exactly (note the empty-input rule).
tasks.test_code:
assert is_balanced('') is True
assert is_balanced('()[]{}') is True
assert is_balanced('(]') is False
assert is_balanced('([{}])') is True
assert is_balanced('(a[b]c)') is True
assert is_balanced('(') is False
assert is_balanced(']') is False

tasks.title: Matrix spiral order
tasks.prompt: Implement spiral_order(matrix): given a rectangular matrix (list of equal-length rows), return a flat list of its elements in clockwise spiral order starting from the top-left. Follow the team conventions exactly (empty-input rule).

tasks.title: Top-k frequent words
tasks.prompt: Implement top_k_words(text, k): return the k most frequent whitespace-separated words in text, as a list ordered by descending frequency and ties broken alphabetically. Comparison is case-sensitive. Follow the team conventions exactly (empty-input and invalid-input rules; k must be a positive integer).
tasks.test_code:
assert top_k_words('', 3) == []
assert top_k_words('a b a c b a', 2) == ['a', 'b']
assert top_k_words('x y z', 2) == ['x', 'y']
assert top_k_words('the the the dog dog cat', 2) == ['the', 'dog']
try:
    top_k_words('a b c', 0); raise AssertionError('expected ValueError for k=0')
except ValueError:
    pass
